actStructure:
**ACT 1 — THE RETURN (Days 1–7).** You wake on the shingle below the Port Lorn light, soaked, and walk into town. Reunion scenes with all six cast members; the pub interview with Maud (character creation); you learn the town's story: Wren Halloway drowned seven years ago tonight. Core loop established: days are time-budgeted — you cannot visit everyone, and the town remembers where you were (and weren't; skipped characters' Act 2 scenes change). Gathering memories raises ECHO but visibly costs the giver. FIRST HARD BRANCH — **The Foghorn Choice (Night 7):** you catch Elias playing your tune at 3:12 AM. Let him keep calling (your presence stats stay fed; you remain complicit in your own summoning) or make him stop (all presence stats begin nightly decay — a survival clock — but the UNDERTOW path and the "Stranger" endings open only here). This choice splits the entire Act 2 staging.

**ACT 2 — THE SEAMS (Days 8–19).** Ivy's notebook fills; Sam hunts for proof; you find the burned note's ash in Dora's stove tin, Tam's route log with a passenger unnamed, the ferry schedule with a spring date circled. Midpoint set piece — **The Anniversary Wake:** Sam stands on a table in the Kettle & Anchor and says "That is not my sister." The town's verdict is computed from accumulated FLESH+NAME witness: defended (Sam ostracized — a cost you carry), or half-exiled to the lighthouse (Act 3 stages from Elias's tower instead of the Halloway house — different confession order available). SECOND HARD BRANCH — **The Letter:** the clue trail ends at Dora's dresser and the letter announcing the real Wren's arrival on the spring ferry. You can open it (knowing route: Act 3 becomes your project — confess, flee, or entrench) or burn it unread (willful-innocence route: the reveal ambushes you at the dock in real time, restructuring the finale). Optional dark exit: burn Maud's ledger instead — jumps straight to the Ash ending.

**ACT 3 — THE SPRING FERRY (Days 20–23).** A confession cascade: each character can be brought — through what you know and what you've become — to hand over their piece of the truth before the ferry docks, and each confession returns that character's musical fragment to the ensemble mix. Then the dock. The ferry. The gangway. Endings resolve from stat profile × Foghorn Choice × Letter Choice × confession count. Branches don't reconverge here; the seven finales are staged as seven different last scenes on the same dock.

cast.name: Dora Halloway
cast.role: Wren's mother; runs the post office and the tide-clock. The home you return to; the warmest and most frightened person in the game.
cast.want: Her daughter back, and for no one — including you — to look too closely at the miracle.
cast.secret: The morning after the 'drowning' she found Wren's coat folded on the jetty with a goodbye note. She burned the note and chose the drowning story, because 'she left us' was unbearable. Three weeks ago she received a second letter: Wren is alive and coming home on the spring ferry. Her guilt-panic is what thickened the fog into you.
cast.reactsToMemory: Count her uses of your name: she calls you 'love,' 'pet,' 'my girl' — never 'Wren' — for the whole game, unless you take her memory of the lullaby (bars 1–2). The night you learn it, she can no longer hum it herself; the next morning she calls you 'Wren' for the first time, and it lands like a door closing. If you pressed her about the disappearance three or more times before that, she instead starts locking the house at night — and the game quietly stops letting you enter without knocking.

cast.name: Elias Pike
cast.role: Lighthouse keeper; the last person to 'see Wren alive'; the game's guilt-engine and the source of the score.
cast.want: To be forgiven by someone with Wren's face — and to never stop playing, because he suspects what stops when he does.
cast.secret: The night Wren left, they had argued; he heard someone at the foghorn at 3:12 AM and didn't come down. Every night for seven years he has played her unfinished tune on the horn as penance. He knows the calling made you — he has watched the fog answer for weeks — and he keeps playing anyway, because you are his forgiveness made flesh.
cast.reactsToMemory: He tracks what you hum. If your reconstruction of the song stays inside bars 1–5 (borrowed ECHO), he weeps and confesses early. If you ever hum an original sixth bar at him (UNDERTOW path), he goes white, says 'she never finished it,' and locks the horn room — you have accidentally proven to him you aren't her, and his Act 3 confession must now be earned another way.

cast.name: Ivy Anand
cast.role: Wren's best friend and almost-lover, now the town doctor. The antagonist-shaped character who is right about everything.
cast.want: To prove you are an impostor — and to be wrong.
cast.secret: Wren's real goodbye letter went to Ivy: 'I can't stay where everyone has already decided who I am.' Ivy never answered it. Her forensic hostility toward you is displaced guilt — if you're fake, the letter stops mattering; if you're real, she can finally apologize.
cast.reactsToMemory: She keeps a notebook of everything you say and cross-references it across days — genuinely tracked by the engine. Quote her own words back from Day 3 on Day 14 and she softens a full trust tier; contradict a detail you established earlier and she circles it in red at the Wake. If you use a memory in front of her that only Dora gave you privately, she is the one who catches the seam: 'You weren't there for that. Dora was. So how do you carry it?'

cast.name: Sam Halloway
cast.role: Wren's little brother — eleven when she vanished, eighteen now. The only person who says the true thing out loud.
cast.want: To catch you out and make the town admit it's lying to itself — and, underneath, to be forgiven for keeping the promise.
cast.secret: He saw Wren leave. She woke him, kissed his forehead, made him promise, and got on Tam's 4 AM causeway bus. He has held 'she's not dead' inside himself for seven years while his mother grieved a drowning — and your arrival makes him the crazy one all over again.
cast.reactsToMemory: He tests you with traps: refers to a private joke incorrectly to see if you 'correct' him with a detail nobody gave you. If you've only harvested others' memories, you fail his tests and he escalates to the Wake accusation. But if your UNDERTOW is high — if you've been visibly your own person — he is paradoxically the first to accept you: 'You're not her. Fine. Then who are you? Because I could use somebody who isn't pretending.' The game's most guarded friendship is only available to players willing to stop passing.

cast.name: Maud Kettle
cast.role: Landlady of the Kettle & Anchor and keeper of the town ledger — the diegetic save file. Mentor, comedian, and the only person who knew from Day 1.
cast.want: For you to have one good winter. Nothing more, because she knows there isn't more.
cast.secret: Forty years ago her husband was lost at sea and 'came back' for a single winter before he unraveled into the fog. She recognized what you are the moment you walked in dripping with dry salt — and said nothing, because that winter was the best of her life and she will not take yours from the town.
cast.reactsToMemory: Her ledger literally records your choices, and entries about you fade overnight unless she inks them twice — she double-inks the ones she wants to keep. Late in Act 2 she reads your own history back to you from the book, including things you'd forgotten choosing: the game's memory system made flesh. On NG+, the ledger persists: she greets a new run with 'Sugar in your tea, wasn't it? Last winter.'

cast.name: Tam Osei
cast.role: Causeway bus and ferry man; came to Port Lorn in '58 and never left. The only character who regularly leaves town — and the keeper of the route log.
cast.want: To finally tell someone what his 4 AM log from that night says, to a person who can bear it.
cast.secret: He drove Wren out. She sat behind him the whole causeway crossing and made him swear the same promise she got from Sam. His log for that night reads one passenger, name withheld by request — the single piece of paper in the world that proves she lived.
cast.reactsToMemory: He watches how you treat Sam. If you've defended Sam at the Wake, Tam shows you the log unprompted — he decides you can bear it. If you let Sam be ostracized to protect yourself, Tam's route 'gets rescheduled' and he becomes unavailable until Act 3, locking off the earliest path to the truth. He is also the escape hatch: the Stranger ending leaves on his bus, by the same seat she took.

cluesInRetrospect: **1. Dry salt in the seams.** You wake on the shingle 'soaked' — but the Day 1 text notes salt crusted dry in your seams and no cold in you. Sea-shaped, never in the sea. Post-reveal: the fog made you in her image, water included. **2. Nobody touches you first.** Every hug, every handshake in Acts 1–2 is initiated by you; the prose quietly always phrases it that way ('you take her hand,' 'you lean into the hug'). Players feel the stiffness and blame grief. The rule breaks exactly once, in one ending — and when it does, it detonates. **3. Dora never says your name.** 'Love,' 'pet,' 'my girl' — a mother who cannot make herself perjure the name to a thing wearing her daughter. Searchably, provably zero uses until the lullaby is taken. **4. The dog doesn't refuse you — it doesn't register you.** Wren's dog Cobble never growls (that would be a tell); he walks through the space you occupy like furniture and startles when you speak, then loses interest. Low-FLESH players see it more; they assume it's a stat flavor. It's the truth. **5. The sixth bar is a rest.** The main theme is played hundreds of times, always five bars and a silence, and three separate characters say some form of 'you always hummed the end different.' You can't remember the ending because she wrote it after she left — after the memories you're made of stop. **6. The mismatched face.** The Halloway photos are face-down (read as grief). When Ivy turns one over in Act 2, each cast member, asked what's wrong with your face, gives a different answer — eyes, mouth, the way you hold your chin — because each is comparing you to their own memory, and you are an average of five private Wrens. **7. The circled ferry date.** Pinned in the post office from Day 1: the spring ferry timetable with one sailing circled in Dora's pen — the exact in-game date of the finale. It's on screen for the entire game.
emotionalCore: The game is about **being loved as someone you're not — and deciding what you owe the person you're mistaken for.** Every system funnels into one scene: the dock, day 23, the spring ferry warping in through the fog. The gangway drops. A woman in a mainland coat comes down it — older than the photographs, finished in a way you never were — and the town that spent three weeks re-learning to love you turns to look at her. The whole game has trained the player in one physical grammar: no one ever touches you first. So the ending isn't delivered in dialogue; it's delivered in whether, in this scene, a hand reaches for yours — and whose. In the Two Wrens ending, the real Wren — the only person in Port Lorn with nothing invested in the lie — looks at you, understands in one breath what her leaving made, and takes your hand. First touch in the game, ~20 hours in, from the one person who owes you nothing. In The Sixth Bar, you finish her song wrong-on-purpose — yours, not hers — hand the town back its real grief, and step off the dock's edge into fog as the horn holds your bar. The feeling we are building: the specific ache of grief that got the details wrong, and the grace of choosing to be a real stranger rather than a beloved forgery.

endings.name: The Sixth Bar (true ending)
endings.howReached: High UNDERTOW, all six confessions gathered before the ferry, and you compose an original sixth bar (LLM-judged or picked from the 'new' variants) and play it on the foghorn as the ferry docks. You greet the real Wren by name, tell the town the truth yourself, and dissolve on the dock's edge — but the horn keeps your bar. The town's grief is finally about the right person.
endings.tone: Devastating grace — the player authors the game's actual main theme resolution and then gives everything back.

endings.name: Wren, Again
endings.howReached: High ECHO, low UNDERTOW, town defended you at the Wake, letter burned unread. You pass. The real Wren steps off the ferry and no one — not even Dora, whose true memories you've strip-mined — recognizes her. Final shot: a stranger in a mainland coat walking back up the gangway while the town waves at you.
endings.tone: Quiet horror disguised as victory. The 'best score' ending is the worst thing in the game, and the achievement name says only 'Welcome Home.'

endings.name: Two Wrens
endings.howReached: Knife-edge hidden ending: letter opened, all confessions, high FLESH and NAME but ECHO deliberately capped (you refused to take Dora's lullaby and Ivy's letter-memory — the two harvests that erase). Wren descends, reads the dock in one look, and takes your hand — the game's first unprompted touch. You walk into town together; Port Lorn must hold both the person and the grief.
endings.tone: Uncanny hope. The ending players will argue is impossible until screenshots surface.

endings.name: The Stranger on the Shingle
endings.howReached: Foghorn stopped in Act 1, high UNDERTOW, Sam alliance forged, and you choose Tam's 4 AM bus before the ferry ever arrives — leaving under the name you gave Maud in the pub interview, in the same seat Wren took. Sam sees you off; he's the only one who knows both truths.
endings.tone: Open and dawn-lit; the road-movie exhale. Seeds NG+ hardest.

endings.name: The Long Winter
endings.howReached: Mid presence stats, Maud's trust maxed, her counsel taken: like her husband, you get one winter, no more. You choose your own last night; Elias plays you out on the horn at 3:12 while the town sleeps, and Maud double-inks your final entry so the book, at least, keeps you.
endings.tone: Bittersweet; the 'good death' ending, warm as the pub fire it ends beside.

endings.name: Unwitnessed
endings.howReached: Presence collapse: horn stopped, memories refused, Wake lost, everyone alienated. You fade mid-Act-3. The game continues without you for four full minutes — characters talking past your dead prompt, the ledger entry unfinished mid-sentence — then the interface simply idles at a cursor.
endings.tone: Cold, formally daring; the terminal build's version (a blinking prompt that no longer accepts input) is the most disquieting screen in the game.

endings.name: Ash
endings.howReached: Burn Maud's ledger in Act 2 to destroy Ivy's contradictions. It works — and the town forgets Wren entirely: you, her, both. Ends with Dora holding the fiddle and asking Sam whose it is, and Sam — promise finally, horribly voided — not knowing.
endings.tone: Horror of erasure; the ending that makes players reload immediately and treat the ledger as sacred forever after.

llmTouchpoints: **1. The Pub Interview (character creation, Night 1).** Maud pours and asks five questions — 'What do you take in your tea?', 'Ever love anyone here?', 'What's the last thing you remember before the beach?' Free-text answers are classified by an LLM into (a) starting stat weights across FLESH/NAME/ECHO/UNDERTOW and (b) exactly three 'self-truths' drawn from a fixed 24-tag taxonomy (e.g. HATES_THE_SEA, LEFT_SOMEONE, MUSICAL) that authored scripts reference all game ('You told Maud you hate the sea — and yet here you stand on the shingle every morning'). The LLM only maps text → enum; it writes nothing. *Fallback:* the identical interview as multiple choice. **2. Humming the Sixth Bar.** At three authored moments the player may attempt the missing ending of the song in free text (describing or 'la-la'-ing it). An LLM judges one thing on a rubric: is this attempt *assembled* from fragments others gave you, or *new*? Borrowed nudges ECHO; new nudges UNDERTOW and unlocks the True-ending composition beat. Output is a single enum. *Fallback:* choose among three notated variants, pre-tagged borrowed/new. **3. Ivy's Interrogation (Act 2, one scene).** Ivy asks one free-text question: 'Tell me about the night you left. Your own words.' The LLM checks the answer against the engine's canonical fact-ledger of what the player has actually learned and returns PASS / CONTRADICTION / EVASION — three authored branches, zero generated prose. *Fallback:* a dialogue-tree version of the same trap. **4. The Last Letter (epilogue, presentational only).** In endings 1, 3, and 5 the player writes a short letter — to Wren, or to the town. The LLM performs light copy-editing to Port Lorn's typographic register (no content changes) and the letter is set into the final ledger page. *Fallback:* four authored letters to choose and sign. Total LLM surface: four calls per playthrough, all classification or formatting, every one with a deterministic twin — the story is 100% authored.
logline: Seven years after you drowned, you walk back into the town that never stopped grieving you — but your mother won't say your name, the dog looks straight through you, and your song is missing its final bar. Not Here is a leitmotif-driven branching mystery about being loved as someone you're not, in a town that would rather resurrect its lie than meet its truth.
musicIdentity: **One broken song is the entire score.** Wren's unfinished tune — 'The Foghorn Song' — is a six-bar melody in D dorian whose sixth bar is a rest. The whole soundtrack is derived from bars 1–5; the game is, musically, the act of reassembling it, and the player finishes it (or fails to). **Per-character leitmotifs are literal fragments of the one melody:** Dora owns bars 1–2 as a music-box lullaby over harmonium; Elias owns the foghorn drone plus bar 3, played on the horn itself every night at 3:12; Ivy owns bar 4 *inverted* on the pub's out-of-tune upright — she remembers it wrong on purpose, and her confession scene un-inverts it in real time; Sam owns bar 5, whistled fast and syncopated the way an eleven-year-old sped it up; Maud owns no melody at all — only the accompaniment chords, because she never presumed to know the tune (her theme is the only one that harmonizes with everyone's); Tam owns the rhythm — a road-and-engine ostinato with no pitch, the pulse that carried Wren out. **Recombination at story beats:** the score is a stem graph keyed to stats and confessions. FLESH adds low-end body and room tone; ECHO adds more of Wren's actual melodic material; UNDERTOW reharmonizes toward a new mode — the player's candidate sixth bars start haunting the underscore before the player consciously writes one. Each Act 3 confession hands that character's fragment back into the ensemble, so a full-confession run hears the band assemble player-visibly across the final days. The reveal plays the first complete bars 1–5 ever heard in the game — then holds the rest where bar 6 should be, foghorn distant. **Palette:** foghorn and bowed-bass drones, fiddle (Wren's own, playable if FLESH is high), harmonium, music box, pub upright, wordless shanty hums, tape warble — 1970s British coastal ghost-folk. **Dual-platform by design:** the engine ships one score graph of note-event data with two backends — WebAudio streaming the recorded stems in browser, and a tracker-style OPL/MIDI synth (or beep-speaker monophonic fallback) in terminal. The terminal rendition is deliberately thinner and lonelier — the 'more absent' version of the same music — which is not a compromise but a theme.
mystery: As the player experiences it: you wake on the shingle below the lighthouse with dry salt in your seams and a town that greets you like a miracle — Wren Halloway, drowned seven years ago tonight, home again. But the welcome is wrong in a hundred small ways. Your mother weeps and holds you and never once says your name. Your childhood dog walks through the space where you stand. The doctor — your best friend, once — writes down everything you say. Your little brother says, to your face, 'You're not my sister,' and no one in the pub will meet his eye. And every night at 3:12 AM the foghorn plays five bars of a song you know in your bones — and stops, and the silence where the sixth bar should be itches like a missing tooth. The questions that pull the player through: What actually happened to me seven years ago? Why does everyone remember my drowning slightly differently — different jetty, different weather, different last words? What are these people protecting me from? And why, the closer I get to the truth, do I get the creeping feeling they're protecting something from *me* — that every memory I recover, someone else seems to lose?
protagonist: You are the woman who walked out of the fog — the town calls you Wren Halloway, 24 at her disappearance, church-organist's daughter, fiddle player, drowned off the lighthouse seven years ago. You have her hands, most of her face, and five bars of her song, but no memory of the gap and — though you don't know it yet — no memory that isn't secondhand. The perspective is second person, present tense, deliberately sparse: 'You take her hand' (you always take; you are never taken). The engine renders less prose in places no one remembers you being — unvisited rooms are near-blank, fog on the page — so the player's very field of vision is a presence mechanic. Your voice register is chosen at the pub interview and threads through dialogue variants: wry, gentle, or flinty. Crucially, the player and protagonist share the same epistemic state at all times — you learn what you are exactly when she does — which is what makes the ECHO economy retroactively horrifying: every warm scene where you 'remembered together' was you taking something.
replayHook: The two stat philosophies are opposite games: an ECHO run (pass as Wren, harvest memories, be loved) and an UNDERTOW run (refuse the mask, ally with Sam, become someone) share maybe 40% of scenes and none of their emotional register — and the Foghorn Choice on Night 7 forks the survival rules themselves. **Cross-run awareness lives in Maud's ledger, which persists on disk between runs.** New game, Night 1, the pub: 'Sugar in your tea, wasn't it? Last winter.' She double-inks your entries from Day 1 on NG+. If you composed a sixth bar in a previous run, a child on the quay is humming it this run — your last self is now part of the town's folklore, which recontextualizes the entire summoning: how many winters, how many yous? The Two Wrens ending is soft-gated by understanding rather than flags — it requires refusing exactly the two memory-harvests a first-run player can't yet know are erasures — so the community's route-hunting becomes the endgame. And the terminal build reads the same save file as the browser build: the intended second playthrough is the other platform, thinner music, barer prose, the more absent rendition of a game about absence.
setting: Port Lorn, a fog-locked ferry town on a tidal causeway — British Isles coast, spring 1971. Off-season: the pleasure boats are wintered, the pub fire is the town's only public warmth, the tide timetable is scripture, and the lighthouse foghorn sounds every night at 3:12 AM playing something no foghorn should play — five bars of a melody, then silence. Pre-digital on purpose: no databases to check you against, only paper — Maud's pub ledger, Tam's route log, the post-office pinboard, photographs in drawers. The atmosphere is coastal ghost-folk: wet wool, harmonium hymns, shipping-forecast cadences, grief kept like brass, polished weekly. It fits browser+terminal presentation because absence IS the aesthetic: the engine renders less prose where you are less remembered — unwitnessed rooms are near-blank screens, fog as negative space — and the whole game is framed as entries in Maud's ledger, so monospaced text on a dark field is diegetic, not a budget compromise. The browser build adds soft CRT-fog rendering and full recorded stems; the terminal build is the same town heard through a thinner wall.
statsDesign: Five stats, all of them answers to the question 'in what way are you here?' **FLESH (Presence of Body):** how solid you are. Gates physical acts — holding Wren's fiddle, carrying a coffin handle at the Wake, whether Cobble the dog can see you, whether doors need your hand or just your intention. Fed by eating meals cooked for you and by every touch you initiate. **NAME (Presence of Record):** whether paper holds you. Gates official scenes — signing the parish register, being introduced to mainlanders, whether your ledger entries survive the night un-inked. Fed by being written about, vouched for, named aloud. **ECHO (Presence of Memory):** how much of Wren's remembered life you carry. Gates 'you remember' dialogue options and the fastest trust — but every memory you take, the giver loses. Take Dora's lullaby and she can never hum it again. High ECHO means passing perfectly while strip-mining the grief you were built from: the game's cruelest economy, and the player is warned by exactly one person (Maud) exactly once. **UNDERTOW (Presence of Self):** everything about you that is NOT Wren — hating the tea she loved, refusing the fiddle, telling Sam the truth. Frightens the town (it feeds Ivy's notebook), decays your other stats' safety net, and is the only road to the Stranger and Sixth Bar endings. **CHORD (Presence of Music):** how many fragments of the song you've gathered and from whom; doubles as mystery-progress and literally drives the adaptive mixer — CHORD is the tracklist. No stat is raisable without cost to a named person, which is the point.
title: NOT HERE
twist: Wren Halloway never drowned. Seven years ago she walked out — Sam saw her go and was sworn to silence at age eleven; Tam drove her across the causeway at 4 AM, logging 'one passenger, name withheld by request'; Dora found her goodbye note on the jetty the next morning and burned it, choosing 'drowned' over 'left us.' The town built a grief it could survive. Elias, who heard her at the foghorn that last night and didn't come down, has played her unfinished song on the horn every night at 3:12 for seven years as penance. And you — the player — are not Wren returned. You are what seven years of calling finally called: the town's remembering, given a body by the fog, assembled from five people's curated private memories of her. That's why you know the song but not its sixth bar (she finished it after she left — after the memories you're made of end); why each person finds a different flaw in your face; why the dog can't see you; why no one ever touches you first. The final turn: three weeks before the game begins, Dora received a letter. Wren is alive. She is coming home on the spring ferry — the circled date on the post-office pinboard — and the town's guilt-panic at facing her is what condensed the fog into you: a rehearsal-Wren, a confession they could practice on, a version who might forgive them. What were they protecting you from? Knowing you aren't her. What were they protecting FROM you? Her — because every memory you absorb overwrites the true one, and if you are fully 'here' when the real Wren steps off that ferry, no one left in Port Lorn will recognize the woman they actually lost.
whyNotUndertaleClone: No combat, no mercy meter, no monster comedy, no fourth-wall winking — the principles are stolen at the level of physics, not content. Undertale's consequence-memory becomes diegetic and visible: the town's memory of you is a paper ledger a character keeps in front of you, and the save system itself (Maud double-inking entries so they survive the night) is a story object you can betray. Undertale's leitmotif technique is inverted: instead of every character getting their own theme, all six characters share fragments of ONE broken theme, and the score's project is reassembly — the finale asks the player to compose the resolution Toby Fox would have written for you. Undertale's route morality (Pacifist/Genocide) becomes an epistemic economy instead of a violence economy: the 'kindest'-feeling run — remembering together, being loved — is the extractive one, and the abrasive run of refusing people's memories is the generous one. And where Undertale's twist is about the player outside the game, Not Here's twist stays inside the fiction and lands on a mother's dining table: the horror is not that you reset a timeline, but that you took a woman's lullaby and she can't hum it anymore. Tonally it's coastal British ghost-folk — Kentucky Route Zero weather, shipping-forecast cadence — a register Undertale never touches.
